title: Not Without My Daughter

jokes.text: George Michael's 'I'm your girl' joke on Bring Your Daughter to Work Day
jokes.setup: George Michael reminds Michael it's Bring Your Daughter to Work Day and says 'I'm your girl'
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals Michael first made this joke when George Michael was six — implying the son absorbed the father's bit and now performs it earnestly
jokes.explanation: The meta-layer is rich: the joke-within-a-joke structure (Michael made the joke, George Michael inherited it) perfectly establishes the episode's theme and George Michael's sad earnestness. The narrator's contextualizing punchline is exemplary AD construction — it retroactively reframes what we just saw and adds pathos.

jokes.text: 'They're out of sanitary napkins in the ladies' washroom' — George Michael, playing along with the daughter bit
jokes.setup: Michael is already uncomfortable with the 'I'm your girl' joke as George Michael has grown older
jokes.punchline: George Michael leans in hard, walking up and announcing there are no sanitary napkins in the ladies' room — a teenager committing fully to the bit
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional joke because George Michael's specific, clinical delivery of 'sanitary napkins' — as a teenage boy trying to be the good daughter — is perfectly character-integrated. It's funny because of HOW committed he is, not the shock of the topic. The narrator's 'Weird kid' underscores it with deadpan economy. Earns its 8+ through layered character specificity and escalation of the running gag.

jokes.text: 'Pretty soon I'm going to have to start wearing a bra. Right, Dad?'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to gently suggest they drop the joke this year because George Michael is getting older
jokes.punchline: George Michael escalates the bit by suggesting he'll soon need a bra, cheerfully doubling down
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the running gag — George Michael going further rather than pulling back — is both character-perfect (his anxious desire to please his dad) and structurally sound. The bra line is specific and unexpected.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I cannot kill this joke.'
jokes.setup: Michael keeps trying to shut down the daughter bit but George Michael keeps escalating
jokes.punchline: Michael's private, defeated admission that he literally cannot end a joke he started 10 years ago
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economic — four words that summarize Michael's specific parental helplessness. It's a great internal monologue joke that works because it's so resigned.

jokes.text: Maeby completes Tobias's therapy patient's sentence: '...HOMOSEXUAL.'
jokes.setup: Tobias's therapy patient struggles to articulate a feeling — 'Does that make me, you know, like some kind of...'
jokes.punchline: Maeby flatly supplies 'homosexual' — the word the patient was obviously reaching for — treating it as a mundane fact
jokes.explanation: Brilliant construction: the patient's hesitation creates the expectation of some profound psychological insight; Maeby's bored, blunt completion is perfectly timed and character-perfect (she's jaded, unsentimental). Then Lucille's follow-up makes it even better — the moment earns an 8 for economy and structural precision.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'She's right though. You probably are a homosexual.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has just blurted 'homosexual' to Tobias's therapy patient; Tobias is embarrassed
jokes.punchline: Lucille calmly agrees with the child's diagnosis, adding another layer of matter-of-fact cruelty
jokes.explanation: Lucille's casual cruelty is one of the show's most reliable comedic engines. Her agreeing with a child's offhand remark — as if confirming the weather — is peak character comedy. The line is also a callback to the season-long Tobias-is-secretly-gay thread. Earns its 8 through character specificity: only Lucille says this, in exactly this tone.

jokes.text: Gob's magic show was filmed for 'Girls with Low Self-Esteem'
jokes.setup: Gob excitedly announces his Spring Break magic act was filmed and is on TV
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the video series is called 'Girls with Low Self-Esteem' — a parody of 'Girls Gone Wild', implying the footage is salacious and Gob performed magic for a spring break exploitation video
jokes.explanation: This is a standout joke. 'Girls with Low Self-Esteem' is a perfect satirical title — it's darker and more honest than 'Girls Gone Wild', commenting on the actual nature of those videos. That Gob is proud of being on it is also character-perfect. The setup (Gob excited about his magic) subverts into a revelation about the video's nature. Rewatch value because the title only gets funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: 'Not really. Kids love boundaries.' — Tobias's post-hoc rationalization after Maeby obeys Michael
jokes.setup: Maeby immediately obeyed Michael when Tobias said she wouldn't
jokes.punchline: Tobias instantly pivots to claim this proves his theory — 'kids love boundaries' — reframing his failure as a win
jokes.explanation: Tobias's ability to make his failures into wisdom is perfectly character-integrated. The pivot is instantaneous and delivered with total conviction — a great character beat that's also a clean joke.

jokes.text: The tape shows Gob's magic act failing — 'The only thing this guy could make fly away was the crowd.' DOUCHE CHILL.
jokes.setup: Gob has been excited about seeing himself on TV doing magic at spring break
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan 'The only thing this guy could make fly away was the crowd'; Maeby's reaction 'Douche chill' — implying the performance is so embarrassing it causes physical discomfort
jokes.explanation: A two-part joke: the narrator's pun on 'make fly away' is sharp and economical, then Maeby's 'douche chill' — a piece of teen slang used perfectly — lands as the button. Gob's oblivious pride makes it cringe comedy as well.

jokes.text: Gob: 'It's not like they used the part where I made the yacht disappear.' / Narrator: 'Sunk the yacht. Blew it up, sunk it.'
jokes.setup: Gob is upset they used the embarrassing part of his act, consoling himself that they at least didn't use 'the part where I made the yacht disappear'
jokes.punchline: Narrator flatly corrects: 'Sunk the yacht. Blew it up, sunk it.' — revealing Gob's 'disappearing' was actually destruction of property
jokes.explanation: The narrator's correction is comedy gold — the flat, matter-of-fact 'Blew it up, sunk it' completely deflates Gob's self-delusion. The repetition ('sunk the yacht. Blew it up, sunk it.') is perfectly rhythmed. Rewatch bonus because it reinforces the yacht-destruction callback.

jokes.text: Gob: 'If you give someone permission to use a tissue, you can't be upset if they blow their nose. Right? I mean, right?'
jokes.setup: Gob tries to argue Michael is partly responsible for the yacht being blown up because he gave permission to use it
jokes.punchline: Gob's tissue/nose analogy is so perfectly stupid — the analogy doesn't hold at all (blowing a nose ≠ blowing up a yacht) — and his desperate 'Right? I mean, right?' reveals he knows it's weak
jokes.explanation: This is among the best jokes in the episode. Gob's logic is internally consistent but externally absurd in exactly the right way — the tissue metaphor is almost reasonable until you realize he's equating nose-blowing with yacht-destroying. The repeated 'Right? I mean, right?' is pitch-perfect: Gob is aware on some level that his analogy fails and is seeking validation anyway. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific — only Gob delivers this with this combination of confidence and desperation. Earns its 8.5 craft score.

jokes.text: Lindsay's 'hand-me-down from Mom' with price tag still on it
jokes.setup: Michael notices Lindsay has a new outfit; she claims it's a hand-me-down from their mother
jokes.punchline: Michael points out the price tag is still attached, catching her lie immediately
jokes.explanation: A functional visual gag — the price tag is a classic comedic device for catching a liar — but fairly conventional. Gets the job done.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Only two of those words describe Mom, so I know you're lying to me.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay calls Lucille a 'sweet old thing'
jokes.punchline: Michael's observation that only two of the three words apply to Lucille — the audience must figure out which two ('old' and 'thing') — is an elegant, compact insult of their mother
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The economy is exceptional — Michael doesn't explain which two words, letting the audience work it out (presumably 'old' and 'thing'). It's a devastating indictment of Lucille delivered with complete casualness. Could become a template ('only two of those words describe X') — highly quotable. The joke works because it trusts the audience's intelligence. Earns its 8+ through near-perfect construction and total economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Unless you're stealing or working, I'm pretty sure it is.' (re: money for clothes)
jokes.setup: Lindsay insists her shopping 'isn't costing Michael anything'
jokes.punchline: Michael lists two mutually exclusive (for Lindsay) options — stealing or working — as the only ways she could have money, implying she's incapable of either
jokes.explanation: Sharp construction — by listing 'stealing OR working' as equally alarming options, Michael reveals that both are considered failures. The irony is that Lindsay has done both (applied for work AND shoplifted), which pays off immediately.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Lindsay applied for a job — shown in flashback with 'Can I have a job application please?' — but was 'relieved when Michael gave her the out'
jokes.setup: Michael accuses Lindsay of either stealing or working; she says 'That's right. I stole it.'
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals she had actually briefly tried to get a job — a moment of desperation — and treated Michael's accusation of theft as a face-saving exit
jokes.explanation: AD's narrator-as-comedian device at its best: the brief flashback of Lindsay sheepishly asking for an application, then being 'relieved' to be accused of shoplifting instead, is deeply character-revealing and funny. Her preferring the criminal accusation is a perfect inversion of normal values.

jokes.text: Gob: 'What'd you use for misdirection?' / Lindsay: 'My ass.' / Gob: 'My ass. You're lying.' / Lindsay: 'No, I'm not.'
jokes.setup: Gob, treating Lindsay's shoplifting as a magic trick, asks what misdirection she used
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's 'My ass' — meaning she distracted guards with her body — causes Gob to mishear or disbelieve it, creating a rapid-fire exchange of cross-purposes
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes from Gob treating theft as a magic competition and the ambiguity of 'my ass' as both a dismissal and a literal answer. The rapid repetition of 'my ass' creates escalating absurdity. Solid mid-tier joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'It was shoplifting, and I'm white. I think I'm going to be okay.'
jokes.setup: Michael warns Lindsay she could end up in prison like their father
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's breezy racial calculus — invoking her whiteness as a get-out-of-jail-free card with total unselfconsciousness
jokes.explanation: A genuinely sharp piece of social commentary disguised as character comedy. Lindsay's casual, matter-of-fact invocation of white privilege — delivered as if it's an obvious, neutral fact — is both funny and pointed. The joke works because her obliviousness IS the critique. Only Lindsay says this; it's perfectly character-integrated. Earns its 8+ as both comedy and subtle satire.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Shoplifting? Cool. Can you take me?'
jokes.setup: Michael is lecturing Lindsay about shoplifting being wrong
jokes.punchline: Maeby, with complete calm, asks if Lindsay can take her shoplifting — treating it as a fun activity
jokes.explanation: Maeby's amorality is always well-deployed. Her treating shoplifting as a fun outing is character-perfect and well-timed as a button to Lindsay's defense of theft.

jokes.text: Buster carrying a purse, asking if it goes with his outfit — 'DOUCHE CHILL' reaction from George Michael
jokes.setup: Buster strolls in holding a purse and asks if it goes with his outfit
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition of Buster's complete obliviousness to gender norms paired with 'DOUCHE CHILL' — a teen reaction showing the gap between Buster and normal social awareness
jokes.explanation: 'Douche chill' as a running gag across the episode is nicely deployed. Buster carrying a purse as a grown man is character-perfect. The moment also underscores the episode's theme of gender performance and manliness.

jokes.text: 'Okay, so you can run faster than me. But you can't make me work.'
jokes.setup: Maeby runs away from Michael at the office building; he chases her
jokes.punchline: Michael's out-of-breath declaration that though she outruns him physically, she can't make him not bring her — a completely losing position delivered as if it's a victory
jokes.explanation: Michael's physical failure paired with moral insistence is a nice character beat. The construction — admitting defeat on one level, claiming victory on another — is solid.

jokes.text: 'See that? Who knew you were such a good little climber?' — Michael complimenting Maeby for climbing to escape him
jokes.setup: Maeby apparently climbs something to escape Michael
jokes.punchline: Michael reframes her escape attempt as a bonding discovery — 'who knew you were such a good little climber' — desperately positive-spinning his complete failure to control her
jokes.explanation: Michael's relentless optimism reframing disaster as opportunity is a reliable character engine. This is a solid mid-tier deployment.

jokes.text: Annyong appears as Lucille's date to the soccer game and says 'She don't want other soccer moms think that she a single mother. She old school. I liked it better when he just said Annyong.'
jokes.setup: Annyong is brought in as a possible escort for Lucille to the soccer game
jokes.punchline: Annyong gives a surprisingly articulate and perceptive explanation of Lucille's social anxiety, which Michael undercuts with 'I liked it better when he just said Annyong' — preferring Annyong's meaningless catchphrase to his actual opinions
jokes.explanation: The joke works on the Annyong running gag (he normally only says his name) but subverts it: when he finally speaks properly, what he says is insightful and slightly unsettling. Michael's preference for the simpler, mute Annyong is a sharp indictment of how the Bluths treat him.

jokes.text: Annyong: 'Go, fatty.' to Buster
jokes.setup: Buster complains that Annyong never cheers him
jokes.punchline: Annyong flatly says 'Go, fatty.' — a technically supportive but viciously insulting cheer
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy (two words) for maximum comic impact. 'Go, fatty.' is technically an answer to Buster's complaint (Annyong IS now cheering him) while being a devastating insult. The deadpan delivery implied for Annyong's character makes it land perfectly. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Buster: 'No, he's not! I came out of you! He didn't!'
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Buster to stop teasing Annyong because 'he's your little brother'
jokes.punchline: Buster's hysterical distinction — 'I came out of you! He didn't!' — is an accurate biological fact delivered with unhinged emotional intensity
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'I came out of you' is both technically accurate and deeply strange — the kind of thing only Buster would shout in this context. His mama-boy psychology and jealousy over Annyong collide perfectly here.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Damn it! Can't you just find somebody else, please, to look like your husband?' / Narrator: 'In fact, there was someone else who looked like Lucille's husband...'
jokes.setup: Michael dismissively tells Lucille to find someone who looks like her husband
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately reveals this is George Sr.'s identical twin Oscar — making Michael's throwaway suggestion both ironic and already true
jokes.explanation: A classic AD structural move: a character says something flippantly and the narrator reveals it's already happening. The cut to Oscar arriving as 'someone who looks like Lucille's husband' — because he literally is his twin — is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And back at the model home, three of the Bluth men struggled with their manliness.'
jokes.setup: We cut to George Michael, Tobias, and Buster at the model home
jokes.punchline: The narrator announces they're 'struggling with their manliness' — perfectly summarizing all three characters' crises (George Michael as 'daughter', Tobias's repressed sexuality, Buster's arrested development)
jokes.explanation: The narrator's line is doing a lot of work efficiently — it simultaneously characterizes all three men and sets up the episode's thematic spine (manliness and performance of gender). The triple application (George Michael/daughter, Tobias/gay, Buster/mama's boy) is elegant.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I know I'm a man. It's his insecurity, not mine.' — talking head about the daughter joke
jokes.setup: George Michael is defending the daughter joke in what appears to be a talking head
jokes.punchline: He claims Michael's discomfort is 'his insecurity, not mine' — but the audience has seen him eagerly doing the bit, making this a complete self-delusion
jokes.explanation: George Michael's total sincerity in misattributing the problem to Michael is both funny and a little sad. His confidence in his own sanity while doing a very strange thing is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I mean, if I have to have a job to impress my daughter. If she needs something that butch.'
jokes.setup: The men are discussing impressing their daughters with toughness; Tobias considers getting a security guard job
jokes.punchline: 'If she needs something that butch' — Tobias's qualifier reveals he finds even security guard work aggressively masculine, a window into his character
jokes.explanation: 'That butch' is peak Tobias — a single qualifier that reveals volumes. Only Tobias would describe a security guard job as requiring consideration of whether it's 'butch enough.' Perfect character economy.

jokes.text: Michael and Maeby's honesty bet: 'I would like to see if you could go one day without telling a lie. Just one whole day without being like your mom.'
jokes.setup: Michael catches Maeby pretending to be asleep
jokes.punchline: His challenge to be honest includes 'without being like your mom' — a pointed insult of Lindsay disguised as parenting advice
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's honesty/lie plot engine. The Lindsay insult is delivered casually as a baseline comparison, which is itself an insult — Michael doesn't even flag it as an insult. Rewatch bonus because the bet pays off in multiple ways.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Good night.' — after Michael offers his respect as a reward for honesty
jokes.setup: Michael offers 'you'll win my respect' as the reward for Maeby going a day without lying
jokes.punchline: Maeby dismisses this with 'Good night' — treating his respect as worthless — until he adds '$50'
jokes.explanation: The economy here is exceptional — 'Good night' in response to 'you'll win my respect' is devastatingly efficient. Maeby's mercenary worldview is perfectly captured in two words. Only improves when Michael adds the cash.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Fine. I'll do it if you do it.' / Michael: 'That-a-girl. This is gonna be fun. I win.'
jokes.setup: Maeby challenges Michael to also go a day without lying
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'This is gonna be fun. I win.' — his smug confidence that he's already winning by being honest establishes dramatic irony, since the plot will immediately test him
jokes.explanation: Sets up the dramatic irony engine beautifully — Michael's 'I win' declaration immediately before he's investigated by police for a murder he didn't commit is structural payoff. Rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Detective: '...the disappearance of Kitty Sanchez. Apparently you're the last person to see her... alive. Or around town or whatever.'
jokes.setup: Detectives arrive at Michael's office on Bring Your Daughter to Work Day
jokes.punchline: 'Apparently you're the last person to see her... alive.' — dramatic pause — '...Or around town or whatever.' The walk-back from 'alive' (implying murder) to 'around town or whatever' deflates the dramatic build perfectly
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed misdirection: the detective builds to a murder accusation with the pregnant pause after 'alive,' then immediately undercuts it with 'Or around town or whatever.' This move — deflating dramatic language into casual indifference — is a signature AD joke type and executed perfectly here. The rhythm of the pause is everything.

jokes.text: Michael: 'She was my secretary, but she left. Or I-I fired her.' — immediately undercutting his own alibi
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to establish he has nothing to do with Kitty's disappearance
jokes.punchline: He immediately corrects himself to admit he fired her — volunteering incriminating information while trying to seem cooperative
jokes.explanation: Michael's honesty compulsion — established in the bet — immediately begins costing him. This is the engine of the episode's main plot, and its comedy comes from how involuntary his self-incrimination is.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I certainly didn't... didn't kill her and drop her in a back bay. Boy, I hope that's not what happened to her. I'm sure it isn't.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to assure the detectives of his innocence while his honesty compulsion keeps sabotaging him
jokes.punchline: He speculates aloud about how Kitty might have died — with the exact method that sounds most guilty — then tries to walk it back with 'I'm sure it isn't'
jokes.explanation: The comedy of someone volunteering specific details of a hypothetical crime they didn't commit is well-mined territory, but AD executes it with genuine character specificity — Michael's honesty compulsion makes him say the one thing he shouldn't. The walk-back ('I'm sure it isn't') is perfectly pathetic.

jokes.text: Detective immediately radios: 'Please send Officer Davis and little Hanna to the back bay.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just volunteered that Kitty might be dead in a back bay
jokes.punchline: The detective immediately dispatches officers — including 'little Hanna' (clearly his daughter, on Bring Your Daughter to Work Day) — to the back bay, treating Michael's self-incrimination as actionable intelligence
jokes.explanation: The joke has two layers: the detective instantly treating Michael's hypothetical as a confession is funny, but the 'little Hanna' detail — implying even the detective brought his daughter to work — is an elegant callback to the episode's theme that adds a visual bonus (a cop dispatching his daughter to a potential crime scene).

jokes.text: Maeby: 'So you killed Kitty, huh?'
jokes.setup: Michael has just finished his disastrous interaction with the police
jokes.punchline: Maeby's flat, casual summary — 'So you killed Kitty, huh?' — treats the situation as unremarkable
jokes.explanation: Maeby's deadpan observation functions as a button on the whole police sequence. Her willingness to believe Michael might be a murderer and treat it as mildly interesting is great character comedy.

jokes.text: 'And apparently a fun one.' / 'Let's go see what's going on in the back room.' / Narrator: 'Were those the last words Kitty ever heard?'
jokes.setup: Michael declares he's honest and has no secrets; a party is clearly happening in the back room
jokes.punchline: The narrator's ominous 'Were those the last words Kitty ever heard?' applied to something so innocent (an office party) is comic horror that also reframes the mundane as potentially sinister
jokes.explanation: The narrator's willingness to treat office party invitations as potentially final words is a great deployment of AD's dark comedy sensibility. Functions as a mystery-thriller parody within a sitcom.

jokes.text: Someone at the office: 'I think that's her right behind you there.' — pointing to Kitty alive on TV; George Michael: 'And that's the boat that Uncle Gob blew up.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been investigated for Kitty's possible murder; asks if anyone has seen Kitty
jokes.punchline: Someone casually points to Kitty alive on a TV screen behind him — deflating the whole murder plotline — and George Michael helpfully identifies Gob's exploded boat in the background
jokes.explanation: The double callback — Kitty alive + exploded yacht visible — is efficiently constructed. George Michael's helpful ID of the yacht while missing the bigger picture (Kitty is alive!) is a nice character beat.

jokes.text: Tobias at mall security interview: 'I do know stage combat if the partner is willing and a bit more petite than I.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is applying for a security guard job, listing his qualifications
jokes.punchline: His only relevant skill is 'stage combat' — theatrical fighting — with the qualifier 'if the partner is willing and a bit more petite than I' — revealing he can only fight in theatrical conditions with cooperative, smaller opponents
jokes.explanation: Peak Tobias — his theatrical background is completely useless for actual security work, and the caveat 'if the partner is willing' for combat is perfect. The phrasing is elevated and specific in a way that's quintessentially Tobias.

jokes.text: Security manager: 'It's gonna be a couple of days before we can get you a gun license, so you'll have to use mine.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has somehow gotten the security job despite his obviously inadequate qualifications
jokes.punchline: The manager casually offers to lend Tobias his personal gun in the interim — a wildly irresponsible and illegal-seeming solution delivered with total nonchalance
jokes.explanation: The casual absurdity of a manager lending his personal firearm to a new employee — without qualification — is a clean absurdist gag. Tobias's wide-eyed 'Wow!' sells it.

jokes.text: Gob to George Michael re: awkward around girls: 'What do you mean? Like if there were three of us and I didn't know where to start?'
jokes.setup: George Michael timidly asks Gob if he was ever 'awkward around girls' — clearly meaning nervousness/shyness
jokes.punchline: Gob interprets it as a logistics question about managing multiple women simultaneously — a complete misread that reveals the chasm between their experiences
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed misdirection. George Michael's tentative question about social anxiety vs. Gob's interpretation as a threesome logistics problem is a perfect character collision — both are completely in character, and the gap between them is both funny and revealing. The follow-up 'not a lot of complaints, if you know what I mean. At least not from the girl.' compounds the joke. Earns its 8+.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Not a lot of complaints, if you know what I mean. At least not from the girl.'
jokes.setup: Gob is answering George Michael's question about awkwardness with girls
jokes.punchline: 'At least not from the girl' — implying someone else DID complain, and Gob is choosing not to examine why
jokes.explanation: The implication that someone other than the girl complained — carefully unspecified — is a great example of AD's oblique dark comedy. Gob's breezy delivery of something somewhat troubling is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You're saying I should just be myself?' — mishearing George Michael's attempts to change subject / 'And he had to drive her home. So I think I did pretty good.'
jokes.setup: George Michael mumbles something to pivot the conversation; Gob mishears it as validation
jokes.punchline: Gob interprets George Michael's exit as agreement, then continues his unsolicited story — 'and he had to drive her home, so I think I did pretty good' — treating having to drive a girl home as some kind of victory
jokes.explanation: Gob's narrative logic — driving someone home counts as victory — is characteristically bizarre. Works as a button on the girls conversation.

jokes.text: Gob asking the pet store: 'How many mice will $13 buy?'
jokes.setup: Gob has taken George Michael on a 'mysterious errand' — supposedly for a magic trick
jokes.punchline: The errand is buying mice with $13 — a precise and slightly sad budget — for what will turn out to be shoplifting misdirection
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '$13' — not '$20,' not 'a lot' — is funny in its precision and suggests Gob's limited budget. The setup for the mice payoff is well-planted. Also a great standalone image.

jokes.text: George Sr. to Buster: 'You were just a turd out there, you know? You couldn't kick and you couldn't run. You know, you were just a-a turd.'
jokes.setup: Buster has gone to prison to complain about his father's ban on organized sports
jokes.punchline: George Sr. reveals there was no ban — Buster was just bad at sports, and he was 'a turd.' The repetition of 'turd' (three times) escalates the brutality of the assessment while also being absurdly undignified language from a father
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Buster's childhood trauma (banned from sports) was actually just his father noticing he was bad at sports — and the word 'turd' used repeatedly — is both funny and dark. Rewatch bonus: recontextualizes Buster's entire sports avoidance storyline.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Prison has destroyed the way you talk.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just called Buster a 'turd' multiple times
jokes.punchline: Buster's only objection is to the vocabulary — not the brutal assessment — treating 'turd' as evidence of prison's corrupting influence rather than addressing the content
jokes.explanation: Buster's fastidiousness about word choice — objecting to 'turd' while not addressing being told he was a terrible athlete — is perfect character comedy. The economy is excellent: this one line tells us everything about Buster's priorities and his relationship with his father.

jokes.text: Buster mentions Oscar at the soccer game; George Sr.: 'Whoa, whoa, whoa — Oscar? My brother Oscar? Oscar's been coming around?' / Guard: 'NO TOUCHING! No touching. No touching.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is about to angrily grab Buster through the visitation barrier
jokes.punchline: The prison guard's 'NO TOUCHING! No touching. No touching.' — a tri-fold repeat that escalates from shout to normal to tired routine — lands as both a physical comedy beat and a running gag
jokes.explanation: A reliable series running gag — the guard's escalating 'No touching' repetitions are funny through pure deployment at the right moment. Not inventive on its own but serves its function well.

jokes.text: 'NO TOUCHING!' / 'Oh!' — George Sr. attempts handshake, gets shocked by the guard
jokes.setup: George Sr. says 'Put her there' to Buster after their agreement
jokes.punchline: He immediately tries to shake hands, immediately setting off the guard's 'NO TOUCHING!' — a triumphant/ironic application of the running gag
jokes.explanation: The 'put her there' setup makes George Sr.'s immediate rule violation funny — he set up the handshake himself. Good structural use of the running gag.

jokes.text: Prison guard/warden: 'Her self-esteem is through the roof.' — after Buster's daughter apparently does something at the prison
jokes.setup: The 'Bring Your Daughter to Work Day' context continues — even the prison guard has brought his daughter
jokes.punchline: The guard's pride that his daughter's self-esteem is 'through the roof' from apparently working at a prison is a dark, absurdist deployment of the episode's theme
jokes.explanation: The callback to Tobias's self-esteem work AND the episode's daughter-to-work theme in a prison context is a nice layered joke. Tobias was on the 'forefront of the self-esteem movement' — now a prison guard's daughter has high self-esteem from working in a prison.

jokes.text: Michael arrives at police station with Maeby; detective: 'I didn't know you had a daughter.' Michael: 'I don't.' Maeby: 'I thought you wanted me to call you Daddy?'
jokes.setup: Michael has brought Maeby to the police station; the detective recognizes her as his daughter
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I don't' is immediately contradicted by Maeby's 'I thought you wanted me to call you Daddy?' — publicly and humiliatingly in front of a detective investigating him
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered: it's a callback to the daughter theme, it's Maeby weaponizing the situation, it puts Michael in the worst possible light in front of investigators. The timing — Michael has just declared he has no secrets — is perfect structural irony.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Are you nuts? Not without a better lawyer.' — re: polygraph
jokes.setup: Detective asks if Michael is willing to take a polygraph
jokes.punchline: Barry's 'Not without a better lawyer' — referring to himself — is a self-deprecating acknowledgment of his own incompetence
jokes.explanation: Barry's self-awareness about being a terrible lawyer is a running character joke. This line is especially well-deployed: he's literally telling his client he needs a better attorney than himself. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Maeby watches the Gob spring break tape and gets 'douche chills' herself — 'Why do I keep getting these bruises?' (a third deployment of 'douche chill')
jokes.setup: The 'Girls With Low Self-Esteem' tape plays at the police station
jokes.punchline: Maeby gets 'chills' from watching Gob's embarrassing performance, mistaking the visceral discomfort reaction for actual bruising — a character joke on top of the running gag
jokes.explanation: The third 'douche chill' deployment diminishes slightly with repetition, but the additional layer — Maeby thinking the reaction is literal bruising — adds a character-specific twist that keeps it fresh.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I do think that we should lie.' / Michael: 'We don't lie in this family.' Maeby's soda errand used to avoid her hearing Michael lie
jokes.setup: Barry advises lying; Michael refuses because (a) family values and (b) the $50 bet with Maeby
jokes.punchline: 'If I lie in front of her, it costs me 50 bucks' — revealing the real reason for Michael's honesty isn't principle but a cash bet with a child
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Michael's moral stance has a $50 price tag attached is perfectly structured irony. He's maintaining the appearance of principle while acting on pure financial motivation — which is exactly what his family does all the time.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Say what you will about America... 13 bucks still gets you a hell of a lot of mice.'
jokes.setup: Gob and George Michael arrive at the department store with their mice; the errand is revealed
jokes.punchline: Gob's expression of patriotism through bulk mouse purchasing — 'say what you will about America' — is a complete non-sequitur that reveals his bizarre logic chain
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the finest one-liners in Arrested Development's run. 'Say what you will about America — 13 bucks still gets you a hell of a lot of mice' is extraordinary because: (1) it implies an ongoing critical conversation about America that no one is having, (2) Gob's specific pride in bulk mouse value as a metric of national greatness is perfectly absurdist, (3) it's delivered with complete sincerity as if it's a profound observation, (4) the specific '$13' callback lands with a delayed-fuse delight. This is the kind of line that becomes a cultural reference within a fandom. Easily the most quotable moment in the episode.

jokes.text: 'We'll see who the sleight-of-hand artist is in this family.' — Gob, preparing to shoplift
jokes.setup: Gob frames his plan to shoplift as a sibling magic competition with Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Gob treating shoplifting as a contest of magical skill — 'we'll see who the sleight-of-hand artist is in this family' — reveals his total inability to distinguish between magic performance and actual crime
jokes.explanation: Gob's competitive framing of an act of theft as a magic rivalry is excellently character-specific — his ego and his magician identity merge into something completely absurd.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Gob, are we shoplifting?' / Gob: 'MICE!'
jokes.setup: George Michael has just caught on that this is shoplifting
jokes.punchline: Gob refuses to answer and immediately launches the plan — 'MICE!' — using the launch as an escape from the question
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing — George Michael's moral question is literally interrupted by the commencement of the crime. 'MICE!' as a non-answer is maximally economic and hilarious. The structure (setup question → crime launch as punchline) is impeccable.

jokes.text: Michael on the phone as chaos erupts: 'Michael, I'm not as bad an influence as you think I am.' / Michael: 'Well, you're not a great one.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is on the phone with Michael while simultaneously her brother Gob is releasing mice in her store
jokes.punchline: Lindsay defends herself as not-bad-an-influence moments before her store erupts in mouse-related chaos caused by her family; Michael's 'you're not a great one' is deadpan understatement
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony — Lindsay defending herself while her store is destroyed by Gob — is well-deployed. Michael's measured response undercuts the drama perfectly.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'That's what I am, Michael. I'm a shoplifter! I've got a whole bag of clothes I shoplifted!'
jokes.setup: Michael calls Lindsay a criminal during a dispute
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's full confession — shouted into the phone in the middle of a police investigation scene — including the detail that she has 'a whole bag' of stolen clothes
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's proud confession is good character comedy — she's turned accusation into an identity she's embracing. The detail of 'a whole bag' is escalation of the shoplifting plot.

jokes.text: Tobias leaps into action as security guard — 'a surprisingly catlike one'
jokes.setup: Gob's mouse plan is working and chaos has broken out; Lindsay yells 'Call Security!'
jokes.punchline: Tobias — who just got the job — appears as security guard and makes a dramatic, apparently impressive physical intervention; the narrator's 'surprisingly catlike' undercuts expectation (we'd expect Tobias to fail)
jokes.explanation: The subversion works because Tobias's physical failure is expected — his success is the surprise. 'Surprisingly catlike' from the narrator sets up the visual gag payoff and characterizes what we'd be seeing on screen.

jokes.text: 'Wow. I guess we didn't need the mice.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has apparently neutralized the shoplifting attempt with impressive physical skill
jokes.punchline: Gob's casual 'I guess we didn't need the mice' — spoken as if the mice were a reasonable plan option that simply proved unnecessary — is completely oblivious
jokes.explanation: The callback to the mice plan — completed with Gob's total matter-of-factness — is a superb button on the shoplifting sequence. Gob treating 'didn't need the mice' as a neutral operational assessment is peak character comedy. High quotability, excellent economy. Earns its 8.

jokes.text: Lucille to Oscar: 'What are they gonna take, the one can of Comet you've got in there?' / Oscar: 'Oh, your tongue is as sharp as your body is still taut.'
jokes.setup: Oscar worries about leaving his camper unlocked with only a hanger for a lock
jokes.punchline: Lucille's withering assessment of Oscar's belongings ('one can of Comet') is immediately reframed by Oscar as flirtation — his 'your tongue is as sharp as your body is still taut' treats her cruelty as charm
jokes.explanation: Two-part joke: Lucille's insult of Oscar's poverty (one can of Comet) is sharp, then Oscar's lovesick reinterpretation of her cruelty as wit/charm reveals his hopeless infatuation. The 'body is still taut' also functions as a middle-aged compliment of dubious sincerity.

jokes.text: Lucille catching Oscar smoking pot at a children's soccer game: 'Oscar, this is a children's soccer game, not Woodstock.'
jokes.setup: Oscar excuses himself to his camper; returns clearly having smoked marijuana
jokes.punchline: Lucille's Woodstock comparison — delivered with her signature imperious disdain — perfectly places Oscar as a relic of a permissive era while establishing her as the opposite
jokes.explanation: The Woodstock reference is effective shorthand for the cultural gap between Oscar and Lucille. Lucille's specific disdain for the comparison — delivered at a children's event — is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Buster discovers Oscar getting high in his camper — reaction shot
jokes.setup: Buster has come to confront Oscar and assert his manliness; he finds Oscar's camper
jokes.punchline: Buster opens the camper to find Oscar smoking — implied by the 'Open.' followed by the no-no-no reaction sequence — the horror of discovery is played for physical comedy
jokes.explanation: Inferred visual gag from 'Open.' followed by Buster's horrified 'NO NO NO NO' reaction. Functional physical comedy but dependent on what's seen rather than heard.

jokes.text: Buster: 'You do not belong here. My mother's been spoken for. You know, I may never have played organized sports, but I AM A MAN.'
jokes.setup: Buster has come to confront Oscar and assert himself; he's building to a declaration of manliness
jokes.punchline: 'I may never have played organized sports, but I AM A MAN' — delivered as a declaration after a scene where he's been carrying purses, calling his mother, and terrified of a hippie uncle
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'organized sports' — the ban that wasn't a ban — makes Buster's declaration richer. His chosen evidence of manliness (never playing sports) is now known by the audience to simply mean he was bad at them. The irony is dense and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Buster: 'No, she's happy. She's just mean all the time.'
jokes.setup: Oscar suggests Lucille isn't happy; Buster defends her
jokes.punchline: Buster's defense of his mother is the most devastating possible insult — 'she's happy. She's just mean all the time' — treating constant meanness as compatible with happiness
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two best jokes. 'She's happy. She's just mean all the time.' is exceptional because: (1) it's Buster's sincere defense — he actually means this as reassurance, (2) it's a devastatingly accurate characterization of Lucille, (3) the separation of 'happy' from 'mean' reframes both concepts, (4) it's only nine words with enormous character and comedic payload. Highly quotable, works completely out of context. Only Buster — with his specific, distorted relationship with his mother — delivers this as an earnest defense. This is the episode's standout quote.

jokes.text: Buster tries to grab the check and pay for Oscar: 'Let me get you. I'm a man. Move.'
jokes.setup: Buster is performing manliness for Oscar; wants to pay as a gesture of dominance/care
jokes.punchline: 'I'm a man. Move.' — the blunt declaration followed by an instruction is Buster's version of masculine authority, completely undercut by context (he's been mama's-boying all episode)
jokes.explanation: A functional character moment — Buster's attempt at assertiveness is endearing and slightly absurd. 'I'm a man. Move.' is a three-word declaration that the episode has already thoroughly undermined.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I wasn't shoplifting, you twit. Yes, thanks to me. No. I WORK here. At least I used to. They fired me because they thought I was helping this idiot shoplift. Also, they said I wasn't a very good salesgirl.'
jokes.setup: The shoplifting chaos has resolved; everyone is accusing each other
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's revelation that she had a job — and was fired from it — escalates with 'also, they said I wasn't a very good salesgirl' added almost as an afterthought, diminishing both her dignity and her attempt at self-defense
jokes.explanation: The payoff of Lindsay's brief employment is perfectly structured — the callback to her humiliating job application, the reason for her firing (her family), and the additional blow of being called a bad salesgirl compound into an escalating joke. The 'also' is key — it's tossed off, making the extra humiliation land harder.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I knew it! I knew you couldn't pull it off. There's only one magician in this family and that is me. And you got caught by a 13-year-old girl.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been fired for Gob's shoplifting attempt that she had nothing to do with
jokes.punchline: Gob — who planned and executed the mice scheme that caused the chaos — claims victory over Lindsay as the superior 'magician' while noting she was 'caught by a 13-year-old girl' (Tobias, who is actually a middle-aged man)
jokes.explanation: Gob's delusional triumphalism — claiming credit for the operation while misidentifying Tobias as a teenage girl — is excellent character comedy. The '13-year-old girl' error about Tobias is a bonus joke.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'It's not like you were there to spend any time with me, Dad. Because I'm not daughter enough for you.'
jokes.setup: Michael scolds Gob for leading George Michael astray; George Michael defends himself
jokes.punchline: 'Because I'm not daughter enough for you' — George Michael's genuine hurt, expressed through the episode's running daughter joke, reveals that beneath the gag is actual emotional need
jokes.explanation: This is the emotional and comedic peak of the episode's A-plot. 'I'm not daughter enough for you' is both a callback and a genuine revelation — the running joke suddenly cracks open to expose George Michael's real longing for paternal attention. It's funny AND affecting, which is the highest form of sitcom comedy. Only George Michael delivers this, and only in this episode after all the buildup. The episode's best use of its running gag.

jokes.text: Michael: 'It's just you're getting a little too manly for Bring Your Daughter to Work Day.'
jokes.setup: Michael responds to George Michael's emotional vulnerability
jokes.punchline: Michael's gentle acknowledgment that George Michael is 'too manly' for the joke — a callback to the episode's manliness theme and a tender version of the running gag
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the running gag — Michael finally articulating what has made him uncomfortable all episode — is well-earned. 'Too manly for Bring Your Daughter to Work Day' is a gentle, slightly absurd resolution to a running joke.

jokes.text: 'I've put a bounty on my own head.'
jokes.setup: Michael realizes he told Maeby that telling the truth would win her $50 — and the truth may incriminate him
jokes.punchline: 'I've put a bounty on my own head' — Michael recognizes that financially incentivizing Maeby's honesty means she'll now truthfully testify against him
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — 'I've put a bounty on my own head' perfectly captures Michael's predicament in six words. The metaphor is unexpected (bounty hunter imagery for a honesty bet) and completely accurate. High quotability. Earns its 8+ for structural elegance and payoff of the episode's central irony.

jokes.text: Maeby lies her way into the detective's office: 'No, it's okay. I'm Detective Fellows's daughter.'
jokes.setup: Maeby needs to get into the detective's office; she's been told to tell the truth all day
jokes.punchline: She immediately lies — she's Detective Fellows's daughter — to a guard who completely believes her and gives her the door code
jokes.explanation: The callback is rich: Maeby lies about being someone's daughter — the episode's core theme — to get into a police station on Bring Your Daughter to Work Day. The guard immediately hands over the door code, making the deception maximally effective.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I said there's a tape of Kitty getting on the boat and that you guys blew it up. I told them the truth, like you told me.'
jokes.setup: Michael panics about what Maeby told the detectives; she reveals she told the truth
jokes.punchline: Maeby has accidentally saved Michael by telling the truth — which proves Kitty was alive on the boat, not murdered — making Michael's honesty bet the unlikely mechanism of his salvation
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of three running threads — the honesty bet, Kitty's disappearance, and Gob's yacht — all converging through Maeby's accidental truth-telling is the episode's plot climax. The irony (honesty saves Michael from the police) is the thematic punch line of the episode.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I'd save your money. Kitty's got a lot of evidence and she thinks you tried to kill her. So you're probably going to need a better attorney.'
jokes.setup: Michael celebrates and prepares to pay Maeby the $50
jokes.punchline: Maeby — a teenager — delivers the most accurate legal assessment in the scene, noting they'll need a better attorney than Barry, while also completely deflating Michael's victory
jokes.explanation: The callback to Barry's earlier self-deprecation ('not without a better lawyer') landing from Maeby's mouth is elegant. A child being the most legally competent person in the room is peak AD. Also sets up future plot threads.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Lo siento.' — after Maeby calls out that they need a better attorney
jokes.setup: Maeby has just told Michael he needs a better attorney, directly in front of Barry
jokes.punchline: Barry's response to this devastating professional critique is 'Lo siento' — 'I'm sorry' in Spanish — delivered to whom? In what context? The randomness of the Spanish apology is the joke
jokes.explanation: Two words that function on multiple levels: Barry's use of Spanish is unexplained, the apology is for being a terrible lawyer but expressed in an inexplicably foreign language, and the non-sequitur nature of 'lo siento' in this context is a perfect character absurdity. Barry Zuckerkorn's haplessness delivered in the most economical form possible. This is an 8+ joke for pure economy and absurdist character integration.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I hate Bring Your Daughter to Work Day.'
jokes.setup: The episode's events have been disastrous in every thread stemming from Bring Your Daughter to Work Day
jokes.punchline: Michael's final assessment — 'I hate Bring Your Daughter to Work Day' — is the episode button that closes the running gag
jokes.explanation: A functional episode button — not surprising but satisfying. The understatement of 'hate' after everything that happened lands as expected. Does its job cleanly.

jokes.text: On the next AD: Tobias loses his security job trying to solve the store's mice problem.
jokes.setup: Tag scene setup — Tobias is still dealing with the mice Gob released
jokes.punchline: Tobias losing a job he'd had for approximately 20 minutes because of his colleague Gob's shoplifting mice is a perfect escalation tag
jokes.explanation: The 'On the next AD' tag is a show staple that typically contains a real joke. Tobias's immediate job loss is an appropriate escalation tag on the shoplifting plot.

jokes.text: On the next AD: Lindsay 'goes with her initial instinct' — shown applying for another job application
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just been fired; her 'initial instinct' is referenced
jokes.punchline: Her 'initial instinct' is apparently to get another job — shown in a flash of her asking for an application — completing the callback to the episode's job application gag
jokes.explanation: The callback to Lindsay's shameful job application — now treated as her 'instinct' — gives the episode tag a satisfying circularity. Character-accurate and structurally clean.

jokes.text: On the next AD: 'Where is everybody?' / 'HEADS UP, FATTY!' — Annyong kicks ball at Buster
jokes.setup: Annyong seeks revenge after the episode's events
jokes.punchline: 'Heads up, fatty!' — Annyong's revenge callback to his own 'Go, fatty' and the soccer ball sequence, now weaponized
jokes.explanation: The evolution of 'Go, fatty' to 'Heads up, fatty!' as revenge is a perfect tag callback. Annyong's revenge arc compressed into two words is maximally efficient and satisfying. The episode ends with the smallest character getting the biggest laugh.

tmdb_overview: Michael takes Maeby to work with him on Take Your Daughter to Work Day. After being slighted by Michael, Tobias, George-Michael and Gob all go to the mall to prove that they are just manly. Oscar takes Lucille and Buster to Annyong's soccer game.
insight: Escalating absurdity reaches fever pitch: 74 jokes in 27 minutes fuels Season 1's tightest finale.
